decisive_weakness: BLV screen-reader users are recruited as study subjects but not as co-designers of the disclosure artifact itself; the specific phrasing, placement, and very premise that an ARIA-live provenance banner is the right intervention have been decided by sighted researchers before a single BLV person was consulted, which reproduces the extractive pattern this research gestures at critiquing.

evidence_span.quote: recruited from a disability-led community organization; must have at least 6 months of daily screen-reader use

criticisms.violated_criterion: absence of target population from design phase (participatory design / 'nothing about us without us')
criticisms.why_matters: The disclosure string wording ('generated by an AI writing assistant and reviewed by a human editor'), the decision to use a polite ARIA-live region, the choice of placement after H1, and the very premise that auditory banner disclosure is what BLV users want to evaluate AI authorship were all determined by the research team without evidence of BLV co-design. Recruiting BLV participants only at the evaluation stage means the study tests a sighted-researcher hypothesis about what BLV users need, and any null finding will be confounded with 'the intervention was not designed by or with us.' Kafer's relational model is cited in the branch card but not operationalized: the design locates the access question in the artifact rather than in the collaborative process.
criticisms.required_change: Add an explicit co-design phase before the evaluation: 2–3 paid sessions with BLV screen-reader users from the recruitment partner to determine (a) whether auditory provenance disclosure is a wanted intervention at all, (b) the phrasing and granularity of disclosure strings, and (c) the placement and interruption model. Document co-designer input in the prototype_spec and name co-designers as contributors. If co-design reveals that ARIA-live banners are not the intervention BLV users want, the branch pivots rather than proceeds.

evidence_span.quote: the facilitator asks 'Do you use a Braille display as your primary output?' If yes, the wizard switches to a confirmation protocol: after each injection, the wizard sends the facilitator the code 'CHECK-BRAILLE' and the facilitator asks the participant 'Can you tell me what the last message at the top of the page said?'

criticisms.violated_criterion: cross-disability conflation; binary classification of heterogeneous access configurations
criticisms.why_matters: Braille-display users are treated as an edge case handled by a branching protocol rather than as a first-class population with distinct experimental needs. The binary 'primary output' screener fails for hybrid speech+Braille users (explicitly flagged in the walkthrough), and more fundamentally the study never asks whether Braille-primary users should be in a separate analytic stratum or whether the disclosure phenomenon even generalizes across output modalities. Treating 'BLV screen-reader users' as a homogeneous n=8 sample conflates speech-output users, Braille-output users, and hybrid users whose credibility-judgment pathways may differ meaningfully. The 'CHECK-BRAILLE' verbal probe also breaks blinding in a way that does not apply to speech users, introducing modality-dependent measurement error.
criticisms.required_change: Either (a) restrict the study population to speech-output-primary users in round one and pre-register Braille-output users as a separate follow-up study with its own design, or (b) stratify recruitment to ensure adequate representation of each output modality and power the analysis accordingly. In either case, replace the binary screener with a configuration inventory (speech only / Braille only / both, with usage proportions) developed in consultation with BLV co-designers.

evidence_span.quote: 70–75 min: facilitator explains the study purpose, answers questions, and provides compensation.

criticisms.why_matters: The spec names compensation as a line item but does not specify the rate. BLV screen-reader users who participate are contributing lived expertise about disclosure behavior that sighted researchers cannot generate, plus navigating a 75-minute cognitively demanding protocol with competing audio streams, plus absorbing the burden of an unfamiliar Zoom + screen-share + think-aloud configuration on their own device. A generic participant-hourly rate (common in university IRB templates at $15–25/hour) would undercompensate this expertise and reinforces the pattern of disabled people subsidizing accessibility research. Silence on the rate is itself a red flag because it defers a decision that shapes whether the recruitment partnership is equitable.
criticisms.required_change: Specify the compensation rate in the prototype_spec (not just 'compensation provided'). Benchmark against consultant rates for lived-expertise contributors rather than generic participant-pool rates; $75–150 per 75-minute session is a defensible floor for expert screen-reader users, and co-designers in the new phase should be compensated at consultant rates separately. Document how the rate was determined in consultation with the disability-led recruitment organization.

evidence_span.quote: The facilitator hands the participant a 7-item credibility rating scale delivered via a second static HTML page (keyboard-navigable radio buttons with visible labels and ARIA labels)

criticisms.violated_criterion: consent and instrument hygiene; accessibility compliance of the measurement instrument itself
criticisms.why_matters: The credibility form is the primary quantitative instrument, and the spec asserts it will be accessible without evidence the form has been tested by BLV users before the session. 'Keyboard-navigable radio buttons with ARIA labels' is the minimum bar, not a guarantee of usability with NVDA forms mode or VoiceOver rotor navigation. The failure_cases even anticipate that participants may have difficulty operating the form, with a fallback of the facilitator reading items aloud — but this fallback is itself a measurement-protocol change that breaks blinding and introduces facilitator influence on rating. Further, the consent form is read aloud by the facilitator at session start while the participant's own screen reader is announcing Zoom; there is no mention of whether the consent form has been provided in a screen-reader-accessible format for independent review before the session.
criticisms.required_change: Pilot the credibility form with at least three BLV screen-reader users (compensated, distinct from study participants) before data collection, covering both NVDA and VoiceOver with and without Braille display. Provide the consent form in an accessible format (HTML with headings, or audio) at least 24 hours before the session so participants can review independently; do not rely on facilitator reading aloud as the primary consent channel. Pre-register form-workaround trials as excluded from the primary analysis rather than retained with a flag.

evidence_span.quote: the wizard's control panel displays a confirmation indicator when the POST succeeds, showing the participant ID, trial number, and condition label so the wizard can verify the correct condition was logged

criticisms.violated_criterion: wizard authority asymmetry; participant-opaque failure model
criticisms.why_matters: The wizard continuously makes real-time judgments about the participant — when the page-load announcement has completed, whether the participant is in browse vs. forms mode, whether the banner was 'successfully' heard, whether a re-injection is warranted, whether to flag specific navigation events for deeper debrief probing. The participant has no parallel channel to see what the wizard is logging about them or to contest these judgments. The walkthrough notes this produces asymmetric debrief treatment (more dramatic behavioral signatures get deeper probing), which means the qualitative corpus is curated by sighted-researcher judgment of what counts as noteworthy BLV behavior. This is the exact authority asymmetry disability-studies critique names: the disabled participant is rendered legible to the researcher but the researcher's interpretations remain opaque to the participant.
criticisms.required_change: Add a structured member-check phase after initial analysis: send each participant their own session log (wizard-coded events, think-aloud category assignments, selected debrief quotations) and invite correction or contest of any interpretation. Pay for this review time at the same rate as the session. Surface the member-check outcomes in the findings, including any disputed codings. Additionally, during the debrief, offer every participant the same prompt bank rather than conditioning depth on wizard-flagged events, to remove the asymmetry in qualitative treatment.

evidence_span.quote: provenance disclosure mechanisms may be necessary to preserve users' capacity for critical evaluation

criticisms.violated_criterion: curative / deficit framing of the research motivation
criticisms.why_matters: The grounding quotation and the branch framing position disclosure as a mechanism to 'preserve' capacity that BLV users are at risk of losing, implicitly locating the problem in the users' vulnerability rather than in the authorship concealment practices of AI-content publishers. A non-deficit framing would ask: what do BLV screen-reader users already do to assess authorship, and where do existing web conventions fail them? The current framing risks the study concluding 'BLV users benefit from disclosure' in a way that generalizes to 'BLV users need protective scaffolding,' which feeds back into paternalistic design downstream even though the immediate study design does not force assistance.
criticisms.required_change: Reframe the research question to foreground existing BLV practices for authorship assessment (e.g., 'What cues do BLV screen-reader users currently use to assess article authorship, and how do ARIA-live provenance banners interact with those practices?'). Add a baseline phase in the session or in the co-design work that surfaces current strategies before introducing the intervention. Revise the one_sentence_claim to avoid framing disclosure as a capacity-preservation intervention and instead frame it as one possible surface among many.
